# The Last Library

In the year 2147, when books had become artifacts and screens held all knowledge, one woman kept turning pages.

Her name was Elara, and she lived in what used to be called a "library"—a word most children had never heard. The building stood alone in a city of glass towers, its stone walls covered in vines that had grown wild for decades.

Every morning, she opened the heavy wooden doors. Every morning, she dusted the shelves. Every morning, she read.

One day, a boy appeared at the entrance. He couldn't have been more than ten, wearing the standard gray suit of the Digital District.

"Why?" he asked, pointing at a book in her hands. "Everything's in the cloud. Why keep these?"

Elara smiled. "Because some things aren't meant to be uploaded. Some things are meant to be held."

She handed him a book. He stared at it, confused by the weight, the texture, the smell of aged paper.

"Read the first page," she said.

He did. His eyes widened.

"What happens next?" he asked.

"You turn the page," she said. "And you discover it together."
